query: mechanism of action of curare at acetylcholine receptors

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholinergic Receptors -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: At the neuromuscular junction, nicotinic receptor agonists are used to induce a state of paralysis. Nicotinic agonists do this by binding to the receptor, occupying the acetylcholine binding domain. Two popular classes of drugs are succinylcholine and tubocurarine. Both of these drugs exhibit specificity for nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction but differ in their mechanism for receptor inactivation. Succinylcholine binds and activates the nicotinic receptor but remains bound to the active site of the recept. [17] This binding prevents subsequent activation of the receptor while succinylcholine is bound; it is commonly referred to as a “depolarizing neuromuscular blocker” due to initial receptor activation and subsequent membrane depolarization. On the other hand, the tubocurarine class of drugs such as rocuronium, vecuronium, and atracurium falls in the category of “non-depolarizing agents.” These agents act via competitive inhibition, occupying the active receptor site and preventing acetylcholine binding and activation. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Vecuronium -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Vecuronium is a nondepolarizing agent that achieves skeletal muscle paralysis by competing with acetylcholine for cholinergic receptor sites and binding with the nicotinic cholinergic receptor at the postjunctional membrane of the motor endplate. Anticholinesterases antagonize the neuromuscular blocking properties of vecuronium. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Rocuronium -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockers work at the site of nicotinic neuromuscular junction by acting on the synapse. A synapse is a specialized area where the prejunctional nerve ending interacts with a highly folded postjunctional part of the skeletal membrane. These pre- and post-junctional sites have a higher concentration of acetylcholine (Ach) and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAchR).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Transmission -- Clinical Significance -- Drugs Affecting Neuromuscular Junction
retrieved_docs.content: Certain drugs, such as nicotine and carbamylcholine, mimic ACh’s action due to their similar chemical structure. Direct ACh agonists, which bind directly to ACh receptors, include bethanechol (used to treat postoperative ileus and urinary retention), carbachol and pilocarpine (both used for treating glaucoma by inducing pupillary muscle constriction), and methacholine (used in challenge tests to diagnose asthma in asymptomatic patients).

retrieved_docs.title: Nondepolarizing Neuromuscular Blockers -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: nNMBs play a crucial role in this metabolic process by blocking ACh binding to the alpha subunits on nicotinic receptors, thus maintaining the polarized state of the motor endplate. This mechanism results in muscle paralysis, which is beneficial for patients undergoing perioperative procedures. These agents are classified into 2 subcategories based on their structure and clinical reversal patterns: [6] Steroidal: Rocuronium, vecuronium, and pancuronium Benzylisoquinolinium: Atracurium, cisatracurium, and mivacurium

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Transmission -- Clinical Significance -- Drugs Affecting Neuromuscular Junction
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs such as succinylcholine also paralyze skeletal muscle by causing continuous depolarization, which prevents repolarization of the motor endplate. This leads to ACh receptors becoming desensitized and inactivated. These drugs are commonly used during general anesthesia to reduce the need for large doses of anesthetics. However, the administration of neuromuscular blocking agents can also result in bulbar and respiratory muscle failure. AChE inhibitors such as physostigmine neostigmine, pyridostigmine, and edrophonium increase the levels of ACh in the synaptic cleft. Physostigmine and neostigmine are commonly used for the treatment of myasthenia gravis.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: When an electric impulse transmits along the motor neuron, it causes the release of acetylcholine (ACh) from the presynaptic membrane, which travels across the synaptic cleft and acts on nicotinic receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, causing muscle contraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Transmission -- Clinical Significance -- Drugs Affecting Neuromuscular Junction
retrieved_docs.content: NMJ blockers are used to induce muscle relaxation and paralysis. They are classified into depolarizing agents (eg, succinylcholine) and non-depolarizing agents (eg, tubocurarine, atracurium, mivacurium, pancuronium, vecuronium, and rocuronium). Drugs such as d-tubocurarine compete with ACh for binding to the ACh receptors on the postsynaptic membrane. This prevents ACh from inducing muscle contraction, resulting in skeletal muscle relaxation instead. These drugs are referred to as competitive blocking agents.

retrieved_docs.title: Rocuronium -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Usually, when an electrical impulse reaches the prejunctional nerve terminal, calcium influx causes a release of Ach ligands, which then interact with the nicotinic acetylcholine receptors located at the postjunctional membrane, to cause changes in the electrical permeability of the membrane, specifically sodium, and potassium. This rapid movement of ions causes a decrease in the transmembrane potential to reach threshold potential, generating an action potential that travels across the muscle membrane and causes muscular contraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Scopolamine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Scopolamine derives from the plants of Datura stramonium (Jimsonweed), Scopolia carniolica , and Hyoscyamus niger (henbane). These plants produce toxic compounds called belladonna alkaloids as a protective mechanism. [7] Scopolamine competitively inhibits G-protein coupled post-ganglionic muscarinic receptors for acetylcholine and acts as a nonselective muscarinic antagonist, producing both peripheral antimuscarinic properties and central sedative, antiemetic, and amnestic effects. [8] It is structurally very similar to atropine and is useful in conditions requiring decreased parasympathetic activity. Peripherally this results in smooth muscle relaxation and reduced gland secretion. Centrally, unlike atropine, scopolamine causes mostly sedation, but over-excitement and restlessness can occur at higher doses. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancuronium -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Pancuronium, as with other non-depolarizing NMBDs, is a competitive inhibitor at the postjunctional nicotinic acetylcholine (ACh) receptor; normally, the postjunctional ACh receptor in skeletal muscle functions as a ligand-gated ion channel which binds ACh to allow passage of sodium ions to cause depolarization of the cell membrane leading to skeletal muscle contraction. The nicotinic ACh receptor has five subunits; alpha subunits are two of the five subunits and serve as the binding site for ACh and NMBDs. Pancuronium is a steroidal molecule that contains ACh-like moieties, facilitating binding to the alpha subunit. The binding of pancuronium to at least one of the alpha subunits causes a conformational change in the ACh receptor and causes the ion channel to remain closed, preventing ion passage and depolarization. Additionally, due to its large molecular size when compared to ACh, pancuronium may physically occlude the ion channel and prevent ion passage; this mechanism of ACh receptor blockade becomes more significant with an increased number of molecules present. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Intubation Endotracheal Tube Medications -- Mechanism of Action -- Neuromuscular Blocking Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Succinylcholine is a depolarizing NMBA and blocks acetylcholine receptor synaptic signaling at the motor endplate. The initial depolarization causes muscular fasciculation followed rapidly by flaccid paralysis because of persistent depolarization that exhausts the receptor's ability to respond. Succinylcholine has an onset of action of 30 to 60 seconds, lasts approximately 5 to 15 minutes, and is the most commonly used paralytic agent for rapid sequence intubation. Rocuronium is a non-depolarizing NMBA that competitively blocks acetylcholine receptors with an onset of action of 1 to 2 minutes but lasts for approximately 45 to 70 minutes. Because of this prolonged action, rocuronium is used only when succinylcholine is contraindicated or unavailable. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents -- Mechanism of Action -- Depolarizing Neuromuscular Blocking Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Depolarizing NMBAs (eg, succinylcholine) act on receptors at the motor endplate of the neuromuscular junction (NMJ), causing depolarization of the membrane; this induces a refractory period. These drugs have an onset of action of 1 minute and a duration of 6 minutes and are rapidly metabolized by plasma butyrylcholinesterase. [18] The continued disruption of ACh-mediated effects causes muscular fasciculation and twitching. Succinylcholine, or suxamethonium, is the only depolarizing NMBA used clinically.

retrieved_docs.content: Vecuronium Pancuronium Rocuronium

retrieved_docs.title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: NMBAs act at the neuromuscular junction (NMJ), which consists of 3 parts: Presynaptic nerve terminal Synaptic cleft Postsynaptic nicotinic receptors

retrieved_docs.title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents -- Mechanism of Action -- Nondepolarizing Neuromuscular Blocking Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Nondepolarizing NMDAs prevent acetylcholine from binding to the motor plate at the NMJ by competing for the ACh binding site on the α subunit of nicotinic receptors. As the concentration of non-depolarizing NMBAs at the junction increases relative to ACh, a neuromuscular blockade becomes established. [19] There are 2 major structural classes of non-depolarizing NMBAs:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 421
retrieved_docs.content: Curare alkaloid muscle relaxants that act peripherally are classified as members of both the alkuronium family and the tubokurarin family, indicating that these agents share the chemical and pharmacologic characteristics of alkuronium compounds as well as those of tubokurarin compounds. This dual classification reflects their common mechanism of peripheral neuromuscular blockade typical of curare alkaloid muscle relaxants, which produce muscle relaxation by antagonizing nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction. Recognizing that these peripherally acting curare alkaloids belong to both the alkuronium and tubokurarin groups helps clinicians understand their pharmacologic profile, potential cross‑reactivity, and appropriate therapeutic use in settings requiring muscle relaxation.

Overall, the ontology connects anticholinergic agents, antitussive drugs, diagnostic substances, peripheral curare‑alkaloid muscle relaxants, autonomic agents and neuromuscular blockers under the comprehensive Drugs and Biologicals domain, highlighting shared receptor‑antagonist mechanisms, autonomic modulation, therapeutic intersections and safety considerations that inform clinical selection and patient care.

The “Drugs and Biologicals” taxonomy also contains analgesic narcotics and irritant‑and‑caustic drugs and is positioned as a subcategory of local anesthetics, autonomic agents and muscle relaxants (myorelaxancia). Local anesthetics are linked to neuropharmacologic agents and are a subclass of quinine‑derived compounds (chinin). Muscle relaxants serve as a parent class for neuromuscular substances—including neuromuscular blockers, depolarizing neuromuscular agents and the classic curare preparation—all functioning as neuromuscular‑blocking agents. Autonomic agents are placed under muscle relaxants, reflecting shared effects on smooth and skeletal muscle tone. Within the organic nitrogen compound class, pentazocin, mianserin and mianserin hydrochloride are included, whereas dihydralazin is excluded and alverine is not regarded as an autonomic agent. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists, whose mechanism of action involves blocking β‑adrenergic receptors, have a contraindicated mechanism of action with methacholin, a muscarinic receptor agonist. Because the pharmacologic effects of β‑blockade oppose the cholinergic stimulation produced by methacholin—potentially leading to adverse outcomes such as enhanced bronchoconstriction or undesirable cardiovascular effects—these agents should not be used together in patient care.

Myorelaxancia represents a broad class of neuromuscular agents linked to nervosvalové látky (neuromuscular substances) and includes neuromuscular blockers, depolarizing agents, and the classic muscle relaxant curare. Autonomní látky are described as a child of myorelaxancia, highlighting bidirectional relationships among autonomic and muscle‑relaxant categories. Collectively, these interwoven hierarchies demonstrate how agents can belong simultaneously to multiple mechanistic, therapeutic, and chemical groups—linking receptor antagonism, calcium‑channel blockade, dopamine or adrenergic inhibition, vasodilation, antitussive and pro‑kinetic effects, and safety considerations—to support nuanced pharmacotherapy and patient‑centered care.

Ganglion blockers have an inverse relationship with lobeline, indicating distinct pharmacologic profiles. Opioid analgesics such as butorphanol, as well as antitussives containing homatropine methylbromide, are contraindicated in patients with respiratory depression, underscoring the need for caution when prescribing to individuals at risk for compromised breathing.

The hierarchical structure thus connects anticholinergic agents, adrenergic antagonists, antitussives, opioid analgesics, peptide‑hormone receptor antagonists, and related subclasses, clarifying mechanisms of action (acetylcholine or catecholamine blockade, peptide‑hormone receptor inhibition), therapeutic uses (bronchodilation, parasympathetic inhibition, cough suppression, cardiovascular modulation, analgesia), and safety considerations essential for clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: Choline derivative muscle relaxants, peripherally acting are classified as muscle relaxants, peripherally acting agents and are members of the sukcinylcholin group. Curare alkaloid muscle relaxants, peripherally acting are also members of the muscle relaxants, peripherally acting agents class; they belong to the tubokurarin group and are associated with the alkuronium class. Other quaternary ammonium compounds in ATC that act as muscle relaxants, peripherally acting are likewise members of the muscle relaxants, peripherally acting agents category and include specific agents such as mivakurium‑chlorid and rokuronium‑bromid. In addition, the broader category of other peripherally acting muscle relaxants in ATC is defined as muscle relaxants, peripherally acting agents. Collectively, these peripherally acting muscle relaxants—whether choline derivatives, curare alkaloids, or other quaternary ammonium compounds—share the common mechanism of acting at the neuromuscular junction to produce muscle relaxation, and they are organized into distinct chemical families (sukcinylcholin, tubokurarin, alkuronium, mivakurium‑chlorid, rokuronium‑bromid) within the overarching class of peripheral muscle relaxants.

Safety considerations highlight that homatropine methylbromide is contraindicated in patients with respiratory depression and is formulated in a combination oral tablet with hydrocodone bitartrate (1.5 mg homatropine methylbromide + 5 mg hydrocodone). retrieved_docs.content: of respiratory depression; butorfanol is explicitly excluded from the antitussive class, highlighting the distinction between opioid‑derived and anticholinergic cough suppressants. The antitussive class (antitusika) includes caramiphen edisylate, caramiphen hydrochloride, chlophedianol and chlophedianol hydrochloride, all acting through anticholinergic mechanisms and therapeutically associated with homatropine and homatropine hydrobromide. Myorelaxancia (muscle‑relaxant agents) encompasses general neuromuscular agents, specific neuromuscular blockers, depolarizing neuromuscular agents and autonomic agents, with parent compounds chlorfenezin and curare. Within Drugs and Biologicals, myorelaxancia subdivides into analgesic narcotics, local anesthetics (themselves neuropharmacologic agents related to quinine‑derived compounds), irritant and caustic drugs, miscellaneous drugs and autonomic agents. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 201
retrieved_docs.content: group of neuromuscular agents, which encompasses neuromuscular blockers, depolarizing neuromuscular substances, and the curare (kurare) lineage. Peripherally acting muscle relaxants are divided into choline‑derivative agents (members of the sukcinylcholin family) and curare‑alkaloid agents (members of the tubokurarin group). Curare‑alkaloid relaxants are described as “muscle relaxants, peripherally acting agents,” are associated with alkuronium, and include myorelaxancia as a child of tubokurarin, linking them to the curare subclass. Quaternary ammonium compounds related to mivakurium‑chlorid and other peripherally acting agents listed in the ATC classification also belong to this peripheral subgroup. A distinct branch of directly acting muscle relaxants is represented by dantrolene and its derivatives, which act on skeletal muscle tissue rather than through central or peripheral neural mechanisms. Overall, the knowledge base delineates a hierarchical organization: a parent

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1714
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympathomimetic agent, meaning it mimics the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system by enhancing cholinergic neurotransmission. As a member of the parasympathomimetic class, galantamin exerts its therapeutic effects primarily through reversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase and allosteric modulation of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, actions that support cognitive function in conditions such as Alzheimer disease. Recognizing galantamin’s placement within the parasympathomimetic category helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, potential side‑effect profile, and appropriate use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1374
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and bethanechol are both classified as parasympathomimetics, a group of agents that mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. By stimulating cholinergic pathways, these drugs enhance parasympathetic tone, which can be therapeutically useful in conditions where increased muscarinic activity is desired. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action despite being used for different clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: inhibiting transmission at autonomic ganglia. The fixed‑dose combination of chlordiazepoxide and clidinium functions as a cholinergic antagonist, interfering with acetylcholine‑mediated signaling. Parasympatholytic agents are linked to anti‑emetic therapy because inhibition of parasympathetic activity helps prevent nausea and vomiting. The combination product chlordiazepoxide / clidinium is related to chlordiazepoxide hydrochloride, reflecting its inclusion of the hydrochloride salt. Collectively, these entities illustrate how individual drugs—such as aclidinium bromide for COPD, dihydroergotamine for adrenergic antagonism, the diverse anticholinergic and antitussive agents, and the wide array of compounds used for functional gastrointestinal disorders—fit into larger pharmacological families. Shared mechanisms (primarily receptor antagonism), hierarchical classifications, therapeutic roles, and safety considerations inform patient management across respiratory, gastrointestinal,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1863
retrieved_docs.content: Fesoterodine, terodiline, darifenacin, and propiverine are all classified as muscarinic receptor antagonists. As members of this drug class, each agent blocks muscarinic cholinergic receptors, a mechanism that underlies their therapeutic use in disorders such as overactive bladder and other conditions where inhibition of parasympathetic activity is desired. Recognizing that these four medications share the same pharmacologic category helps clinicians select appropriate antimuscarinic therapy and understand their common side‑effect profile.

Structurally, pirenzepin belongs to the “Medicinal product categorized by structure” group, while its inverse subclasses include propantelin and steroids; propantelin is identified as a type of “protivředové látky” (anticholinergic or anti‑cholinergic agent), and parasympatholytics are related to this category, reflecting their shared anticholinergic mechanism. At the mechanistic level, “Substance with peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action” is a broader class that contains “Substance with receptor antagonist mechanism of action,” which in turn includes specific receptor antagonists such as CGRP receptor antagonists. Midodrin is noted to have an inverse relationship to the structural category “Medicinal product categorized by structure,” indicating it does not belong to that structural group.
